sentence: Subjective:
HPI: The patient, a 45-year-old male, presented with an ankle injury sustained while playing basketball with his son.
He landed awkwardly and twisted his ankle, resulting in bruising, swelling, and instability.
He has been having trouble walking and feels the ankle is not stable.
He recently returned to working out and does not want to disrupt his progress.
He has a 10-year-old son who is a basketball enthusiast.
Past medical history: NOT MENTIONED
Review of systems: NOT MENTIONED
Current medications: NOT MENTIONED
Objective:
Vital signs: NOT MENTIONED
Physical exam: The patient's ankle is swollen, with exquisite tenderness on the lateral and medial deltoid regions.
There is no epidermolysis, and capillary refill is brisk.
The skin is intact, and there is no malalignment.
Test Results: The patient had an X-ray of the right ankle, which revealed a displaced lateral malleolus fracture at the Weber C level, with no evidence of medial or posterior malleolar fractures.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has a lateral malleolar fracture.
Problem list: Lateral malleolar fracture; suspected instability.
Plan:
I prescribed meloxicam for pain management and swelling.
I advised the patient to ice the ankle and elevate it regularly.
I also provided the patient with crutches to assist with mobility.
I recommended that the patient continue with coaching in April, but to avoid putting weight on the affected ankle.
The patient will need to be out of commission for approximately three months to allow for proper healing.
